subject: Chemistry
title: Lucretius and De Rerum Natura
description: A micro-lesson that makes Lucretius and De Rerum Natura usable.

hook.text: In 50 BC, the Roman poet Lucretius wrote a poem about atoms that was lost for 1,400 years—then changed the world.

buildup.text: 'De Rerum Natura' (On the Nature of Things) explained Epicurean atomic theory in 7,400 lines of Latin verse.

discovery.text: Lucretius argued that atoms move, collide, and combine—explaining everything from rain to consciousness.

twist.text: A single manuscript survived in a German monastery. Found in 1417, it sparked the Renaissance revival of atomism.

climax.text: Galileo, Newton, and Einstein all read Lucretius. His poem planted the seed that grew into modern physics.

punchline.text: A poem about atoms, lost and found. One manuscript helped restart science.

quiz.question: How did Lucretius's poem influence the scientific revolution?
options: Its rediscovery in 1417 revived atomic theory and inspired later scientists
It contained mathematical proofs of atomic weights
It predicted the discovery of electrons and protons
